title: Farmhouse Console Table (Beginner, Indoor/Outdoor)
summary: A trestle-leg console with X-braced ends, a center leg of its own, and two slatted shelf levels — the one joint that takes care is a hand-traced lap where the Xs cross.
description:
Three leg assemblies carry this table: two X-braced end frames and a plain center leg standing on its own small shelf rail. The X is the only joint in the whole build that isn't a straight butt-and-screw, and it's simpler than it looks — dry-fit the two crossing 2x2s inside the leg frame, trace where they overlap onto BOTH pieces with a pencil, then cut the lap on only ONE of the two boards. The other stays whole. That's the entire technique; no notch depth gauge or dado stack required, just a saw and a traced line.

The center leg is built and squared as its own small unit before it ever meets the ends — legs to a top rail first (working upside down so the top edges land flush), then a lower shelf rail supported on scrap to get the reveal right, then flipped onto its side so the shelf attaches perpendicular to the legs instead of parallel to them. Getting that perpendicular orientation right is what keeps the finished shelf level instead of racked.

Once all three leg units stand on a flat surface about 30" apart, they're tied together lengthwise with 2x2 stretchers, screwed in at a slight angle so the screw threads act like hooks instead of pivot points — square screws pull straight out under load, angled ones don't. Do this step off a garage floor if you can; most garage floors slope slightly to a drain, and a trestle base assembled on a slope looks fine until the table's sitting in the living room.

The tabletop is three 2x6 boards laid side by side and screwed up from underneath — no visible fasteners on top — and the shelf below is slatted 2x6 with a 1" gap between boards, which is both a design cue and a place for dust and crumbs to fall through instead of collecting.

tools.note: All the crosscuts, including the 24° X-brace angles.
Optional for joining the tabletop boards edge to edge before attaching — fine without one too.
Squares the X-brace overlap trace and checks the leg assemblies as you build.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft (furring strips work)
materials.note: Legs, X-braces, and the stretchers tying the three leg units together.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft or stud length
materials.note: Tabletop and the two shelf levels.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelf support rails and the center leg's own top and lower rail.

cutList.note: Four per end assembly, two end assemblies.
Both ends cut at 24° off square, ends ARE parallel, long point to short point. Two per end assembly; only one of each crossing pair gets the lap notch.
Attached to the X-braces from outside; carries the lower shelf slats at each end.

cutList.part: Center leg top and shelf rail
cutList.note: One at the top (flush to the legs), one as the lower shelf, elevated 1-1/2" on scrap while attaching.
Laid side by side, screwed up from underneath.
Split between the lower shelf and the center leg's mid shelf, laid with a 1" gap.

steps.title: Build the two end leg frames
steps.body: Build two end leg sets from the 2x2 legs. A scrap 2x2 block clamped under the bottom shelf position as you assemble helps you land the height consistently on both frames.

steps.title: Fit the X-braces
steps.body: Dry-fit the two X-brace pieces inside one end frame, crossing them the way the diagram shows. Trace the overlap onto BOTH pieces with a pencil, then cut the lap notch on only ONE of the two — the other stays a full, uncut board. Repeat for the second end frame.

steps.title: Attach the X-braces and the shelf rail
steps.body:
Attach the full-length X piece to the inside of the leg frame first, then the notched piece, matching the pencil lines you traced. You may need to drive a screw at a slight angle to seat the second piece — glue and clamp is fine here too, since the shelf rail added next locks everything in place.

Screw the 2x4 shelf support rail across the outside of the X, tying the whole end frame together.

steps.title: Build the center leg as its own unit
steps.body:
Build the center leg upside down: attach all four legs to the top 2x4 rail first, top edges flush. Flip right side up, then add the lower 2x4 shelf rail, propping it on a scrap block to elevate it 1-1/2" off the bottom of the legs.

Turn the whole unit on its side to attach the shelf rail — it needs to sit perpendicular to the legs, not parallel, or the shelf comes out racked instead of level.

steps.title: Stand and tie the three leg units together
steps.body:
Set all three leg units on a flat, level surface about 30" apart. Working with a second pair of hands helps here. Tie them together lengthwise with the remaining 2x2 stock, driving screws at a slight angle so the threads grip like hooks rather than pivoting under load. Glue every joint.

Do this on a level floor, not a garage slab — most garages slope gently toward a drain, and a trestle base built on that slope reads level right up until it's in the room it's going to live in.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: If you have a pocket-hole jig, join the three tabletop boards edge to edge first; if not, that's fine too. Lay the boards on a flat surface, center the assembled leg frame on top, and screw up from underneath into the tabletop — no fasteners show on the show face.

steps.title: Lay the shelf slats
steps.body: Add the shelf slats to both shelf levels with a 1" gap between each board, two screws per joint. Sand every edge, then finish the whole piece — this design works stained for an entryway or left for exterior-rated finish outdoors.

images.alt: Farmhouse-style console table with X-braced trestle legs and a slatted lower shelf
